item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The second image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it seems to come from WDIV-TV, with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, it has been removed. The first image is cc-compliant as it comes from Citrus County Sheriff Office and thus falls into Public Domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's a fake doctor with a very…limited version of the Hippocratic Oath, it seems. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
mc-brain-guyver.
+1

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded:
I kinda thought it was something like that, except what popped into my head was that he

spoilers, maybe
– hide block

was adding the brain tissue all over the place. Just tons of brain filling his ribcage almost to bursting, with little nervy tendrils snaking up to his head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a lot of quite a few other SCPs (can't remember which ones exactly) but it's still good enough to warrant an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is that? It seems like an arbitrary gripe. I feel the article flows much better with the recovery information slowly giving away the situation, rather than telling you everything up front. If the article told you in paragraph two that this guy replaces peoples' brains with scrap electronics the entire (well-written) recovery section becomes redundant. Some articles work better in one configuration than another, trying to shoehorn them into a preferred style only limits what you can do with them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point. By "rough" combinations I intended to suggest that the subject's colour perception was kind of like looking through a crappy 4-bit colour monitor. I don't know how you could conclusively prove that someone's visual perception is set up that way beyond administering a colour perception test - which would likely reveal an inability to distinguish finer gradations between the three primary colours. This would make recognising three-dimensional objects as we normally do a bit challenging, but not impossible; the ability to distinguish between faces was just an example of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, important point!

Subject was able to identify and distinguish between faces, but claimed to able to see only in rough combinations of blue, red and green.

That's literally how we see. I can kind of sort of picture what you mean if I'm thinking of it like a broken monitor showing me big 3-part pixels, but when I try to resolve the colors together, the metaphor falls apart. Saying, for example, "That thing over there is roughly a combination of (so much blue, so much red, and so much green)," you've literally described normal color vision (e.g., "That thing over there is teal."). Maybe the object is blurry and undetailed or the color is indeterminate, but that's still how our eyes work.

I'd need a better description to understand how they see, and why it is abnormal. Do they only see colors and faces, not shape or detail? Do they somehow have three separate perceptual streams for red, green, and blue, and how in the hell does that come out?

item.title: Petrograd said it.
item.content:encoded:
All of what this person said, ditto from me.

I understand that for concise writing, you want to hit the points hard. I, however, was immediately removed from the SCP when I read the interview with these blunt character representations (they read like a superego and an id clashing).

This interviewer… I've met some graduate students who overtime got the nuances of interviewing but initially, they're rough around the edges, like this guy. Any assessor would tell you that you need to build rapport before going into what we in the mental health field call 'reality-testing'. This doctor went right into the reality-testing with a sociopathic, narcissistic genius; it's not a necessity because he doesn't immediately present with delusions. Then again, I assume this is the initial assessment based on the fact that it's labeled 01 but it could be their fifth meeting for all I know.

Also, if a guy like this had always been rejected for what he believed was his rightful place amongst intelligent people, you bet that he would be defensive as heck. The doctor wouldn't even need to go into his rant about his qualifications; a simple derisive 'mmhmm' would be enough to set off one of my patients and it would for this guy.

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded:
Huh… that… ehh…

Alright, I am officially dumb. Thanks Mac, now you will live your life knowing you made me discover that I am dumb.

Just joking, but dude, that's clever…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except for the bit where he has grafted stolen tissue into his own brain, probably.

Besides, even if he is the carrier of anomalous memes, the way to contain them is to contain him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-049 already plays Surgeon Simulator.

But I definitely want to compare 2230 to the other medical skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sounds like the guy from Surgeon Simulator went a bit nutso.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the narrative presented here, but at the moment, if we want to be technical, this guy is not an SCP. His procedures are, but there's nothing (explicitly, from the Foundation's perspective) anomalous about him as a person. I mean, he does have techno-medical-fu, but there's no proof that's him being anomalous and not just an antech virtuoso.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really, really dig this. I'm a sucker for homebrew mad science skips. ^_^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah. That does make a difference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I originally thought he was Anesthesiologist when I saw the picture and noticed he was wearing glasses. Then I read the rest of the article. Still, it's possible (and it would be awesome if he was)

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded: FWIW, I also thought what MacLeod thought was going on.

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded: We're not using THAT word, though.

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded: Huh, that's an interesting theory. I thought he'd been eating them.

item.title: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

When he says he knows how to get into the heads of his competitors, he's saying he's been taking chunks of their brains out and putting them into his own skull. He likely does the same with his patients. If the Foundation X-Ray'd this guy, they'd find that his head is bigger on the inside, or something similarly weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the plus side, I really enjoyed this guy's abilities, history, and the implications on the end of the interview log.

On the minus side, I really don't see how the pictures add anything to the article, and I feel… it seems like the interviewer was unnecessarily condescending in order to give the SCP a reason to rant, rather than that part of the dialogue feeling organic.

Ultimately, neutral vote. I really like a lot of the ideas involved, but it didn't quite gel for me. The pictures were… jarring, and not in a good way. Made the whole thing feel more like a crime blotter than a containment document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Explaining the acquisition/recovery of the SCP before its anomalous effects is one of the biggest pet peeves of mine, and earns this an immediate downvote on that alone.

That aside, I felt like this doesn't do anything that particularly impresses me with the other doctor skips we have on the site. I couldn't get into what it does or the character of the anomaly, so it just felt weakened for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my defense, that was added after I asked this question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. I got vibes of that surgeon who only operates on machines mixed with just about every other abnormal doctor skip I've ever read. I'll agree the last line of the interview is great, and it's keeping me from a downvote, but at the same time, something about the presentation just irks me. Maybe it's the fact that we only have circumstantial evidence and his own admission that he did these surgeries? There just doesn't seem to be anything inherently anomalous about him. I dunno, it's really bothering me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. I like the idea of the horror if someone was constantly told they suck at something, got frustrated, and tried to do the thing, but worked against all common sense (well I guess only sometimes). +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's not allowed to watch TV anymore because he was working on turning his TV into a stun-ray.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True. Object class updated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who sees him as the annoying younger brother of Dmatix's plague mask doctors?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is not that original, but it is very well done anyway. The amount of research in surgery is palpable and does not feels unnatural, also the interview flows preety smoothly.

The only thing that bothers me is the amount of questions left hanging. As far as I can see, there are three "you-will-never-know" things: How the guy does what he does? What he meant with "get in the heads of his competitors"? What he does with the brain tissue?

I personnaly think it is a little overwhelming. Other than that, it is a solid +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well… according to the Object Classes page:

if you lock a living thing in a box and forget about it, it will eventually suffocate or starve to death, and that's not a good outcome.

So, the mere fact you need to feed him, make him Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is he not allowed to watch tv anymore, because he isn't cooperating? Or is there another reason for that. Either way, I'm glad you're classifying the people he operated on as anomalous now. This article is just fun for me in a weird way. I'm sure some parts of it have been "done before," but I love the idea of an anomalous autodidact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right! I've made some fixes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even though he's sapient, I don't think any amount of brain surgery will get him out a giant metal box that's locked twice from the outside. If there's no chance of him breaching containment, or at least getting dangerously close to doing so, he can be considered Safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The end of the interview is wonderfully creepy. Question though, shouldn't the object class be euclid? The guy's sapient and sentient and can possibly do brain configuring stuff to himself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, reminds me of that one guy from the Futurama episode where Hermes gets robot upgrades.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why aren't the patients being contained as well? Until the Foundation understands how they're even still alive, they're pretty much instances of SCP-2230-01 or something to that effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of a human version of 212.
